metadata.title: Guideline on the clinical evaluation of anticancer medicinal products

rules.rule_text: Mass-balance studies (in vivo radiolabelled dose studies) are strongly recommended for anticancer drugs before marketing authorisation, to support understanding of clinical pharmacology and drug-drug interaction assessment.
rules.verbatim_quote: mass-balance studies are strongly recommended (CPMP/EWP/560/95/Rev.1).

rules.rule_text: Food interaction studies must be performed prior to Phase III. The administration in fed or fasted state must be investigated and a rationale for administration in fed and/or fasted state must be provided.
rules.verbatim_quote: Food interaction studies should be performed prior to phase III and administration in fed or fasted state should be investigated and a rationale for administration in fed and/or fasted state should be provided.

rules.rule_text: The potential for drug-drug interactions must be assessed. If in vitro data indicate that the anticancer product will give rise to, or be a victim of, important drug interactions, these must be investigated in vivo as far as possible.
rules.verbatim_quote: The potential for drug-drug interactions should be assessed. If in vitro data indicate that the anticancer product will give rise to, or be a victim of, important drug-interactions, this should as far as possible be investigated in vivo.

rules.rule_text: For regulatory submissions, biomarker investigations must be accompanied by a full description of: (1) the pharmacological rationale and functional role of the biomarker; (2) the intended purpose and context of use; (3) pre-analytical sample collection and analysis considerations; (4) the analytical method including assay performance characteristics; and (5) data supporting clinical validity.
rules.verbatim_quote: Biomarkers investigations, development and validation in the context of drug development for regulatory submissions should be accompanied by a full description and provision of: The pharmacological rationale and functional role of the biomarker; The intended purpose and context of use of the biomarker as well as the hypothesis regarding the relationship between the biomarker and the drug's effects/outcomes; The pre-analytical considerations including sample collection and analysis; The analytical method by which the biomarker is measured, including the performance characteristics of a biomarker assay(s)/test(s); The data supporting the clinical validity.

rules.rule_text: Centralised testing to determine biomarker status is recommended for confirmatory/pivotal studies. When changes to biomarker assays occur between clinical phases, or more than one assay is used, evidence of concordance between assays must be provided.
rules.verbatim_quote: Centralised testing to determine biomarker status is recommended for confirmatory/pivotal studies, while local testing could be considered as a secondary analysis. In cases where changes to clinical trial assays were performed or where more than one assay was used during the development, evidence of concordance should be provided.
rules.exceptions: For established assays with assured standardisation, local testing alone may be sufficient.

rules.rule_text: When a biomarker is essential for the safe and effective use of a medicinal product, early co-development (or close-knit development) of the companion diagnostic and the medicinal product, with thoroughly planned performance studies, is required.
rules.verbatim_quote: In cases where the biomarker measurement is essential for the safe and effective use of a medicinal product, early co-development (or close-knit development) of the companion diagnostic and the medicinal product and thoroughly planned conduct of performance studies are highly recommended, following applicable regulatory guidance, as appropriate.

rules.rule_text: If a continuous biomarker cut-off is used for patient selection in pivotal studies, thorough justification of the cut-off value is required. Cut-off values must be defined based on data about the functional relationship between marker and outcomes, and must be validated prospectively. If the cut-point may need to change based on Phase III data, independent clinical data to validate the change is required.
rules.source_section: Section 5.6. Biomarker development and validation in confirmatory studies
rules.verbatim_quote: If the biomarker used for patient selection is essentially a continuous marker and a cut-off is used to classify patients as biomarker-positive or negative, thorough justification of the adequacy of the cut-off value is required. Any cut-off values should be defined based on data about the functional relationship between marker and outcomes, and any cut-off values should be validated.

rules.rule_text: For cytotoxic compound Phase I trials, the main objectives must include: identification of Maximum Tolerated Dose (MTD), Dose Limiting Toxicity (DLT), and Recommended Phase II Dose (RP2D) for defined schedules and modes of administration; characterisation of frequent side effects and target organ toxicity in relation to dose and schedule; and initial PK characterisation.
rules.verbatim_quote: Maximum Tolerated Dose (MTD), Dose Limiting Toxicity (DLT) and recommended Phase II dose (RP2D) should be identified for defined schedules and modes of administration. Frequent side effects and target organs for toxicity should be characterised as regards relationship to dose and schedule. Severity, duration and reversibility should be determined. Initial characterisation of pharmacokinetics including dose and time-dependencies.

rules.rule_text: Minimum requirements for safety evaluation in Phase I oncology trials include: assessment of symptoms, physical examination, ECG, blood and urine laboratory analyses, and radiological assessment as appropriate. Toxicity must be graded according to a generally recognised system (e.g., NCI CTCAE). ECG monitoring as part of routine is recommended even in the absence of QTc signals in preclinical studies.
rules.verbatim_quote: The minimal requirements for evaluation of adverse effects include assessment of symptoms, physical examination, ECG, blood and urine laboratory analyses and radiological assessment as appropriate. The toxicity should be graded according to a generally recognised system, e.g. the National Cancer Institute's (NCI) Common Terminology Criteria for Adverse Events (CTCAE).
rules.exceptions: If there are no QTc signals in preclinical studies, no dedicated QTc studies are expected, but ECG as routine monitoring is recommended.

rules.rule_text: For non-cytotoxic and molecularly targeted agent (MTA) dose-finding studies, the dose-finding strategy must address not only safety endpoints but also determine an optimal biologically active dose (OBD). DLT and MTD definitions for MTAs must account for toxicities occurring beyond Cycle 1 (prolonged and late-onset toxicities), as standard cytotoxic DLT definitions focused on acute Cycle 1 toxicity are not applicable.
rules.verbatim_quote: In particular in the case of dose-finding for molecularly targeted agents (MTAs), the dose-finding strategy should not only focus on safety endpoints, but also on determining an optimal biologically active dose (alternatively termed 'optimal biological dose' or 'optimum biologic dose'). Standard definitions for cytotoxic agents, typically focused on acute toxicities in Cycle 1, may therefore not be applicable. Lower grade toxicity over longer periods of time that affect tolerability and the possibility of maintaining the intended dose intensity may need to be addressed in the DLT and MTD definitions.
rules.exceptions: Standard cytotoxic DLT definitions remain applicable to conventional cytotoxic agents.

rules.rule_text: In Phase II oncology trials, ORR should be documented according to international standards (RECIST, Volumetric RECIST or WHO criteria). In single-arm studies, external independent review of tumour response is encouraged. Data on duration of response, TTP/PFS, confirmed ORR and available OS data should be reported.
rules.verbatim_quote: ORR should be documented according to international standards (e.g. RECIST, Volumetric RECIST or WHO criteria). External independent review of tumour response is encouraged, according to the objectives of the trial. Data on duration of response, TTP/PFS, confirmed ORR and available data on OS should normally be reported.
rules.exceptions: Modifications to response criteria may be appropriate but must be justified (e.g. pseudoprogression for checkpoint inhibitors).

rules.rule_text: The reference therapy in confirmatory trials must be the best available evidence-based therapeutic option. The choice of reference regimen must be justified and declared/reported prior to randomisation. When the choice is at investigator discretion, the selection must be as detailed as possible in the protocol with stratified randomisation.
rules.verbatim_quote: The choice of reference regimen should be justified and normally this regimen should be selected based on the best available evidence-based therapeutic options. When the choice of the reference regimen is at the discretion of the investigator, the choice of regimen should be declared and reported prior to randomisation, and the choice at patient level should be as detailed as possible in the protocol.
rules.exceptions: BSC or investigator's best choice is acceptable when no established reference therapy exists.

rules.rule_text: Overall survival (OS) is the most convincing primary efficacy endpoint in oncology confirmatory trials and is considered the most important efficacy endpoint. When PFS or DFS is selected as primary endpoint, OS must be reported as a secondary endpoint and there must be no relevant negative effects on OS (in most cases showing trends towards superiority).
rules.verbatim_quote: Generally, demonstrated favourable effects on OS are, from both a clinical and methodological perspective, the most convincing outcome of a clinical trial, and OS is often considered the most important efficacy endpoint to establish a positive balance of benefits and harms. If PFS or DFS is the selected primary endpoint, OS should be reported as a secondary and vice versa. When OS is reported as secondary endpoint, the estimated treatment effect on OS should ensure that there are no relevant negative effects on this endpoint, in most cases by showing trends towards superiority.
rules.exceptions: A large PFS effect, long expected survival after progression, or clearly favourable safety profile may reduce the need for precise OS estimates at approval.

rules.rule_text: If the study is conducted open-label (unblinded), this requires: use of independent tumour assessment review; conduct of sensitivity analyses; and other measures to limit potential bias related to the open-label nature. The Sponsor and study personnel must remain blinded to treatment allocation as much as possible.
rules.verbatim_quote: If the study has to be conducted open label, this has implications with respect to the choice of study endpoints, independent review, conduct of sensitivity analyses and other measures to be undertaken to limit potential bias related to the open-label nature of the trial. The Sponsor and the study personnel are expected to remain blinded to treatment allocation as much as possible.
rules.exceptions: Double-blind design is preferred; open-label is acceptable when blinding is not feasible.

rules.rule_text: For studies with PFS/DFS as primary endpoint, symmetry of tumour/disease assessment schedules and methods across trial arms is paramount. Adherence to protocol-defined assessment schedules is essential and deviations must be reported.
rules.source_section: Section 7.6.3. Time to event analyses and assessment of response and progression
rules.verbatim_quote: For studies with PFS/DFS as primary endpoint, symmetry with respect to study visits and assessment schedules (i.e., frequency and methods of tumour or other disease assessment) across the arms of the trial is paramount. Adherence to protocol-defined schedules is essential and deviations should be reported.

rules.rule_text: Proposals for early stopping for efficacy in Phase III trials must be pre-specified and supported by evidence that prolonging the study would not significantly change the benefit estimate. Early stopping is justified only if the benefit is so important that the control arm is no longer acceptable for all patients matching inclusion criteria.
rules.verbatim_quote: Proposals for early stopping for efficacy should be pre-specified and receive support from evidence demonstrating that prolonging the study would not significantly change the estimate of benefit. This is in addition only justified if the benefit established from the early analysis is so important that the control arm is no longer acceptable for all patients matching inclusion criteria.

rules.rule_text: In non-inferiority oncology trials, the non-inferiority margin for PFS (a composite endpoint) must consider the effect of the reference treatment on all types of events (death, new metastases, progression of target lesions, clinical progression), including description of the reference regimen's effect on each component when available.
rules.verbatim_quote: For a PFS endpoint, which can be considered a composite endpoint, the discussion of a non-inferiority margin should consider the effect of the reference treatment overall but inference should also include a discussion on each type of events (death, new metastases, progression of target lesions, clinical progression) including description of the effect of the reference regimen on each component when available.

rules.rule_text: Confirmatory trial patient population must be characterised by relevant tumour (stage, grade, target expression, other biomarkers) and treatment history (prior therapy, responsive/resistant/refractory). Important selection criteria and uncertainties related to generalisability from the study population must be reflected in the product information.
rules.verbatim_quote: The study population is expected to be characterised by relevant tumour (e.g. stage, grade, target expression, other biomarkers of importance for prognosis and/or tumour sensitivity), treatment (e.g., prior therapy, responsive/ resistant/refractory as appropriate), and patient characteristics (e.g., performance status, co-morbidity, organ, dysfunction, etc.). Important selection criteria or uncertainties related to generalisations from the study population should be reflected in the product information.

rules.rule_text: When allogeneic HSCT is a foreseeable treatment option, criteria for HSCT must be defined as far as possible in the protocol and reasons for performing or not performing HSCT must be captured in the CRF. Patients undergoing HSCT must be followed for OS and EFS as randomised.
rules.source_section: Section 7.5.1. Haematopoietic stem cell transplantation, methodological considerations
rules.verbatim_quote: these criteria should be defined as far as possible in the protocol and reasons for performing or not performing HSCT should be captured by the CRF. it is normally expected that patients undergoing HSCT are followed for OS and EFS as randomised.

rules.rule_text: Older patients and frail patients with clearly impaired performance status must be included in clinical studies when they are expected to be part of the treatment population. Data from older patients must be available for PK analyses and description of the safety profile including dose reduction, dose delay, and concomitant treatment consequences.
rules.verbatim_quote: Whenever older patients are expected to be treated with the new medicinal product in clinical practice, the clinical studies program should enrol a sufficiently large number of older patients, including those with co-morbidities, to enable a benefit – risk assessment. Data from older patients should be available for pharmacokinetic analyses. Description of the safety profile should include aspects of severity of the adverse events profile and consequences, e.g. dose reduction, dose delay or initiation of concomitant treatment.
rules.exceptions: If benefit-risk cannot be assessed with reasonable certainty in older patients, this should be reflected in labelling and post-approval studies may be needed.

rules.rule_text: EU Paediatric Regulation (EC) No 1901/2006 requires consideration of paediatric development early during the development process to ensure timely access for neonates, infants, children, and adolescents to innovative treatment, even for paediatric cancers.
rules.verbatim_quote: the EU Paediatric Regulation (Regulation (EC) No 1901/2006) requires consideration of paediatric development early during the development process to ensure timely access for neonates, infants, children and adolescents to innovative treatment.

rules.rule_text: Non-randomised single-arm trials (SATs) for marketing authorisation must be duly justified, including a predictable course of the disease, a large treatment effect on endpoints such as ORR and duration of response translating to true clinical benefit, and acceptable toxicity. Long-term efficacy and safety must always be collected unless otherwise justified.
rules.verbatim_quote: Resorting to non-randomized trials (e.g. SAT) should be duly justified (including e.g. a predictable course of the disease in combination with a large treatment effect on endpoints such as ORR and duration of response reasonably likely to translate in true clinical benefit, and acceptable toxicity). Long-term efficacy and safety should always be collected unless otherwise justified.
rules.exceptions: Acceptable when RCT is not feasible; remaining uncertainties must be addressed post-authorisation.

rules.rule_text: When using external controls, the criteria to filter external patients to match the experimental arm must be pre-specified prospectively in the protocol to avoid convenient post hoc selection after the endpoint has been observed in the experimental arm. External control patients must represent the current standard of care.
rules.verbatim_quote: It is imperative that these steps are pre-specified prospectively in the protocol to avoid any convenient selection of external controls once the endpoint has been observed in the experimental arm.
rules.exceptions: External controls are only acceptable when treatment effect is dramatic and the usual disease course is highly predictable.

rules.rule_text: For master protocol studies (basket, umbrella, platform) intended to generate pivotal data for marketing authorisation, scientific advice is strongly recommended due to their complexity. The general methodological and statistical considerations of this guideline apply, including Type I error control and biomarker validation.
rules.verbatim_quote: it is strongly recommended to apply for scientific advice when such designs are considered for the generation of pivotal data for marketing authorisation.

rules.rule_text: For basket trial pooling across histologies ('pooling of baskets'), a strong rationale for homogeneous treatment effect based on mechanistic data (e.g., driver mutation), pre-clinical data, and pharmacodynamics data must be provided upfront and supported by the totality of clinical data. The sponsor must justify that interaction with tumour site or histology is negligible.
rules.verbatim_quote: a strong rationale to support a homogeneous treatment effect has to be provided upfront based on mechanistic rationale (e.g., targeting a driver mutation), pre-clinical and pharmacodynamics data, which need to be supported by the totality of clinical data from the basket study. In particular, sponsors must justify and make it convincingly plausible by clinical and/or pre-clinical data that the interaction with tumour site or histology is negligible and this should also be supported by the final results.

rules.rule_text: All toxicity data must be collected and graded by severity according to NCI CTCAE. Exclusion of assumed disease-related events from collected safety data — even if based on reasonable assumptions — is not allowed, as it may hamper the ability to detect drug-related relationships.
rules.source_section: Section 9.4. Safety data collection, analysis and reporting
rules.verbatim_quote: All toxicity should be described, including cumulative toxicity. Exclusion of assumed disease-related events from collected data, even if based on reasonable assumptions, may hamper the ability of detecting a relationship (also) with the drug, and is therefore not allowed.

rules.rule_text: All marketing authorisation applications must include cumulative adverse event rates from pivotal studies at the specified time points of 3 months, 6 months, and 1 year to facilitate regulatory safety assessment.
rules.source_section: Section 9.4. Safety data collection, analysis and reporting
rules.verbatim_quote: All marketing authorisation applications should include cumulative adverse event rates from the pivotal study(ies) at the specified time points 3 months, 6 months and 1 year, in order to facilitate regulatory safety assessment.
rules.exceptions: If time on therapy is significantly shorter or longer, additional or alternative time-points should be considered.

rules.rule_text: AEs leading to dose reduction, interruption, and discontinuation must be reported by relatedness according to the investigator. Laboratory abnormalities (e.g., cytopenias, liver enzymes) leading to dose changes or interruption should preferably also be reported with summary laboratory AE terms.
rules.source_section: Section 9.4. Safety data collection, analysis and reporting
rules.verbatim_quote: It is furthermore recommended that AEs leading to dose reduction, interruption and discontinuation are reported by relatedness according to the investigator. Laboratory abnormalities such as cytopenias or liver enzymes that lead to dose changes or -interruption should also preferably be reported with the summary term laboratory AEs.

rules.rule_text: For immunomodulatory agents including checkpoint inhibitors (anti-CTLA4, anti-PD-1, anti-PD-L1), awareness and monitoring of potential immune-related adverse events — including diarrhoea/colitis, rash, mucositis, liver toxicity, hypophysitis, pneumonitis, and other endocrinopathies — is required.
rules.source_section: Section 9.4.2. Dose reductions and other consequences
rules.verbatim_quote: For immunomodulatory agents such as checkpoint inhibitors, awareness and monitoring of potential development of immune-related adverse events such as diarrhoea/colitis, rash, mucositis, liver toxicity, hypophysitis, pneumonitis and other endocrinopathies are important.

rules.rule_text: For compounds known or suspected to cause long-term immunodeficiency, monitoring for opportunistic infections for up to one year after the end of therapy must be considered.
rules.source_section: Section 9.4.2. Dose reductions and other consequences
rules.verbatim_quote: For compounds known or suspected to cause long term immunodeficiency, monitoring for opportunistic infections for up to one year after the end of therapy should be considered.

rules.rule_text: The SmPC Section 4.8 ADR frequency categories for anticancer drugs must be based on all-causality adverse event frequencies (irrespective of investigators' assessments of relatedness), not solely on investigator causality assessments.
rules.source_section: Section 9.9. Presentation of adverse drug reactions in the product information
rules.verbatim_quote: the frequency categories in the tabulated list of adverse reactions should therefore be based on the frequencies of all-causality AEs (i.e. irrespective of investigators' assessments of relatedness).

rules.rule_text: For paediatric oncology studies, adverse events must include reporting of any observed effects on organ maturation, growth, and development, including fertility. Some long-term aspects will require further post-authorisation follow-up.
rules.verbatim_quote: For studies in the paediatric population, adverse events should include the reporting of any observed effects on organ maturation, growth and development, including fertility. Some of these long-term aspects will require further follow-up in the post authorisation setting.

rules.rule_text: When radiation therapy is part of the treatment context, information on concomitant or sequential use of the investigational agent with radiotherapy must be collected throughout the entire study programme, including dose, fraction, target/field and time. Safety data collection must address radio-sensitisation and 'radiation recall' phenomena.
rules.source_section: Section 9.6. Safety issues related to radiation therapy
rules.verbatim_quote: Information on concomitant or sequential use of the medicinal agent with radiotherapy should therefore be collected throughout the entire study programme, including data on dose, fraction, target/field and time. The safety data collection and reporting should address radiotherapy specific items such as radio-sensitisation and 'radiation recall'.

rules.rule_text: Extended safety data collection, including off-therapy and on-new-therapy periods, should be included in the study design for anticancer drugs, especially for maintenance therapy, when analysis of PFS2 is needed, or when reversibility of an important ADR is of interest. The collection time must be sufficient to capture increased symptomatology and decline in wellbeing associated with disease progression and ADRs of next-line therapy.
rules.verbatim_quote: Extended safety data collection, including off-therapy and on-new-therapy, may therefore be included in the study design, even if not chosen as the primary analysis cut-off for safety outcomes. The collection time should be sufficiently long to allow capture of both the increased symptomatology and decline in wellbeing associated with disease progression, as well as the ADRs of next-line therapy.
rules.exceptions: Not mandatory for all studies; scope may be limited to specific ADRs of interest.

rules.rule_text: In the temporal perspective on safety, for key tolerability events, safety must be described by treatment cycle with measurements of incidence, prevalence per period, time-to-event, and duration of event (including by grade). Cumulative incidence data alone is not sufficient to describe the safety profile of anticancer agents.
rules.verbatim_quote: In addition to standard reporting of adverse events based on cumulative frequencies by toxicity grade, complementary measurements are required for a thorough understanding of the safety profile of a given anticancer drug. Measurements such as incidence and prevalence per period of time or per treatment cycle, time to event, and duration of event (including by grade) should normally be considered.
